coredata.dc:title: Smartphone-integrated dual-emission fluorescence sensing platform based on carbon dots and aluminum ions-triggered aggregation-induced emission of copper nanoclusters for on-site visual detecting sulfur ions
coredata.dc:creator: Yin, Chenhui
Liu, Ting
Wu, Meng
Liu, Haochi
Sun, Qijun
Sun, Xue
Niu, Na
Chen, Ligang
coredata.dc:description: A ratiometric fluorescence strategy was proposed based on carbon dots (CDs) and self-assembled copper nanoclusters (CuNCs) driven by Al3+ ions for S2− detection. Si-CDs/CuNCs@Al3+ exhibits blue and red emission under single excitation. Interestingly, the red emission of the CuNCs was regularly quenched while the blue fluorescence emission of the CDs was preserved after continuous addition of S2−. The fluorescence spectrometer-based S2− linear range is from 0.5 to 40 μM, with a low detection limit (LOD) of 0.16 μM. The fluorescence response of Si-CDs/CuNCs@Al3+ to S2− exhibits a distinct color change process (red to pink to blue), implying feasibility of visual analysis. A portable fluorescence sensing platform was established using the color-to-value conversion function of a smartphone for accurate visualization and quantitative identification of S2− without spectrometer. The fluorescent test strips prepared with Si-CDs/CuNCs@Al3+ can conduct on-site visual analysis of S2− in the water environment more conveniently and quickly. The linear range of S2− detection based on the smartphone-integrated test strip sensing platform is 1–40 μM, and the LOD is 0.42 μM. This work provides a new horizon for target on-site analysis in environmental samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Sulfur ions
Ratiometric fluorescent probes
Smartphone-integrated test strip
On-site analysis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescent sensing of S2− using spectrophotometer
Detection of S2− in actual water samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Tianjin Key Laboratory of Food Quality and Health
Tree Genetics and Breeding Innovation Team
Tianjin University of Science and Technology

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This project was funded by the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities ( 2572021AW42 ), the Project program of Tianjin Key Laboratory of Food Quality and Health, Tianjin University of Science and Technology, P. R. China (No. TJS202102 ), Fundamental Research Funds for Provincial Colleges and Universities in Heilongjiang Province ( 2021-KYYWF-0583 ), 111 Project ( B20088 ), and Heilongjiang Touyan Innovation Team Program (Tree Genetics and Breeding Innovation Team).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Preparation of Si-CDs/CuNCs@Al3+ and visual detection of S2− with a smartphone-integrated test strip sensing platform.
TEM image of CuNCs (A), CuNCs@Al3+ (B), and Si-CDs (C). FT-IR spectrum of CuNCs@Al3+ and Si-CDs (D).
Full (A) and high resolution of Cu2p (B), Al2p (C) XPS spectra of CuNCs@Al3+. Full (D) and high resolution of C1s (E), Si2p (F) XPS spectra of Si-CDs.

UV–vis absorption, fluorescence excitation, and emission spectra of CuNCs@Al3+ (A) and Si-CDs (B). Emission spectra and photo of Si-CDs, CuNCs@Al3+ and Si-CDs/CuNCs@Al3+. Fluorescence spectra of CuNCs under different Al3+ concentrations (D). PL intensity trends of CuNCs with different Al3+ concentrations (E). The fluorescence lifetime of CuNCs before and after adding Al3+ (F).

Fluorescence spectra for CuNCs@Al3+-S2- and CuNCs–S2- (A). UV spectra of S2−, Si-CDs/CuNCs@Al3+ and Si-CDs/CuNCs@Al3++S2−, fluorescence emission spectra of CuNCs@Al3+ (B). Fluorescence decay curves of CuNCs@Al3+ before and after addition of S2− (C).
Fluorescence spectra of S2− detected by ratiometric fluorescent probes (A) and linear relationship between I458/I610 and different concentrations of S2− (B).

(A) Schematic of S2− detection using smartphone. (B) The linear relationship between the B/R of the probe solution and different concentrations of S2−. (C) The color change of fluorescent strips under 365 nm UV lamp. (D) The linear relationship between the B/R of fluorescent strips and different concentrations of S2−.

I458/I610 of Si-CDs/CuNCs@Al3+ in presence of substances (red), and subsequently added S2− (blue). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

head.ce:title: Smartphone-integrated dual-emission fluorescence sensing platform based on carbon dots and aluminum ions-triggered aggregation-induced emission of copper nanoclusters for on-site visual detecting sulfur ions

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Resource Utilization, Key Laboratory of Forest Plant Ecology, Northeast Forestry University, 26 Hexing Road, Harbin, 150040, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Forest Plant Ecology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Resource Utilization, Key Laboratory of Forest Plant Ecology, Northeast Forestry University, 26 Hexing Road, Harbin, 150040, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Tianjin Key Laboratory of Food Quality and Health, College of Food Science and Engineering, Tianjin University of Science and Technology, Tianjin, 300457, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Tianjin Key Laboratory of Food Quality and Health
College of Food Science and Engineering
Tianjin University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Tianjin Key Laboratory of Food Quality and Health, College of Food Science and Engineering, Tianjin University of Science and Technology, Tianjin, 300457, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, Jiamusi University, 258 Xuefu Street, Jiamusi, 154007, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Pharmacy, Jiamusi University, 258 Xuefu Street, Jiamusi, 154007, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A ratiometric fluorescence strategy was proposed based on carbon dots (CDs) and self-assembled copper nanoclusters (CuNCs) driven by Al3+ ions for S2− detection. Si-CDs/CuNCs@Al3+ exhibits blue and red emission under single excitation. Interestingly, the red emission of the CuNCs was regularly quenched while the blue fluorescence emission of the CDs was preserved after continuous addition of S2−. The fluorescence spectrometer-based S2− linear range is from 0.5 to 40 μM, with a low detection limit (LOD) of 0.16 μM. The fluorescence response of Si-CDs/CuNCs@Al3+ to S2− exhibits a distinct color change process (red to pink to blue), implying feasibility of visual analysis. A portable fluorescence sensing platform was established using the color-to-value conversion function of a smartphone for accurate visualization and quantitative identification of S2− without spectrometer. The fluorescent test strips prepared with Si-CDs/CuNCs@Al3+ can conduct on-site visual analysis of S2− in the water environment more conveniently and quickly. The linear range of S2− detection based on the smartphone-integrated test strip sensing platform is 1–40 μM, and the LOD is 0.42 μM. This work provides a new horizon for target on-site analysis in environmental samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Sulfide plays an important role in many industrial, biological, environmental and catalytic processes. However, the excessive accumulation of sulfide in the ecosystem will cause various hazards to the environment and organisms, which has attracted much attention [1]. It is widely distributed in natural water and wastewater as the major sources of pollution [2]. Studies have shown that exposure to certain concentrations of S2− can lead to loss of consciousness, irritation of the respiratory mucosa, allergic skin reactions and even other more serious negative effects [3,4]. Therefore, the detection of S2− in the water environment is of great significance for monitoring its discharge and environmental pollution.

At present, many analytical methods have been used to detect S2−, such as ion chromatography, electrochemical method, fluorescence method, chromatography, etc [5–11]. Fluorescence method is widely used because of high sensitivity, simple operation and instrument. However, single-emission fluorescence signal is easily interfered by the external environment and other factors. In order to avoid these interferences affecting the accuracy of the experimental results, ratiometric fluorescence analysis is a good choice. The method is favored by measuring the change in the fluorescence intensity of two or more fluorescent materials at different emission wavelengths to avoid background interference. CuNCs have received extensive attention as a new low-cost fluorophore [12–16]. However, CuNCs are prone to oxidation and have a very low fluorescence quantum yield (about 0.1%), so it is imperative to improve their quantum yield and stability. CDs are also very competitive fluorescent materials due to their stable luminescence properties and good water solubility [17–20]. At present, several ratiometric fluorescence strategies of CuNCs and CDs have been reported [21,22].

Aggregation-induced emission (AIE) has attracted considerable attention since its first discovery in 2001 [23]. Unlike common fluorescent materials, which typically quench when aggregated, AIE materials emit bright light when aggregated. When the poor solvent is added to the good solvent, the AIE effect occurs. In addition, polyelectrolytes can also trigger the AIE effect. For example, Goswami et al. found that chitosan can trigger glutathione-stabilized gold cluster AIE [24]. A heparinase detection strategy was developed based on heparin-induced Ag2S quantum dot AIE [25]. The generation of the AIE effect can greatly improve the fluorescence quantum yield. This broadens its application in bioimaging and sensing due to its simple operation and adjustable brightness [26–28]. Herein, we found that Al3+ can trigger the AIE effect of CuNCs. This provides the possibility to construct an analytical method for S2− sensing.

However, current methods mainly rely on instrument detection, which cannot achieve rapid on-site visual analysis of the detected objects. To facilitate the on-site visual detection of analytes, paper-based sensing has entered the public eye. More and more fluorescent materials-based test strips have been developed for on-site detection of targets [29–32]. However, most fluorescent test strips are only capable of qualitative analysis. To achieve the quantitative detection of analytes, colors of fluorescent strips are extracted via a smartphone and converted into values. Finally, the purpose of quantitative detection is achieved.

Based on the above research background, we prepared a smartphone platform based on ratiometric fluorescence test strips of silicon-doped CDs and Al3+-enhanced CuNCs composites to realize rapid visualization of S2− in aqueous environment (Fig. 1 ). With the increase of S2− concentration, CuNCs@Al3+ combined with S2−, resulting in fluorescence quenching, while Si-CDs remained unchanged. The color of Si-CDs/CuNCs@Al3+ changed from orange to light pink to light purple and finally blue with the addition of S2−. The corresponding red, green, blue (RGB) values can be obtained by smartphone software. Based on this color change, an analysis model B/R can be established to visually detect S2−. In addition, the fluorescent test strips prepared with ratiometric fluorescent probes (Si-CDs/CuNCs@Al3+) can more conveniently and quickly conduct on-site visual detecting S2− in river water. Compared with the visual analysis method in solution, the fluorescent test strips prepared in this experiment not only has a long storage time and is easy to carry, but also has a good LOD, which is instrumental in visual analysis of fluorescent test strips to detect pollutants.

Citric acid, isocyanatopropyltriethoxysilane, glutathione, sodium sulfide, aluminum chloride, copper sulfate were all purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). Ions (K+, Na+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Ba2+, Zn2+, Al3+, Cl−, NO3 −, Ac−, F−, Br−, I−, SO4 2−, PO4 3−, HPO4 2−, H2PO4− and CO3 2−) were derived from their salt-forming compounds and purchased from Rhawn (Shanghai, China). All reagents were used directly without purification.

The surface topography of Si-CDs and CuNCs@Al3+ were observed by JEM-2100 TEM (JEOL, Tokyo, Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was conducted on the PHI 5700 ESCA System Analyzer (PE, Minnesota, USA) to analyze elements composition of Si-CDs and CuNCs@Al3+ powders. Fourier Transform Infrared spectra (FTIR) was measured on FTIR-360 spectrometer (Nicolet, Madison, WI, USA) to analyze elements composition. The fluorescence test was conducted on F-4600 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan). UV–vis absorption spectrum was obtained on TU-1901 spectrophotometer (PERSEE, Beijing, China). Fluorescence lifetime was performed on the FLS-920 instrument (Edinburgh, UK).

Briefly, citric acid (0.2 g) was placed in a Teflon liner and dissolved in distilled water (10 mL). Subsequently, isocyanatopropyltriethoxysilane (400 μL) was added, stirred evenly, and put into a autoclave. The reaction kettle was placed in an oven and reaction at 180 °C for 10 h. Si-CDs were obtained by filtration with 0.22 μm microporous filter. Subsequently, the supernatant was filtered and dialyzed using dialysis membrane (3500 Da) for 24 h.
2.4 Preparation of CuNCs@Al3+

The synthesis of CuNCs was based on literature [33]. With stirring at room temperature, glutathione (10 mL, 50 mg/mL) was slowly added to copper sulfate solution (10 mL, 10 mM), the solution gradually became cloudy, and continued to add dropwise to gray-black until finally milky white. The pH was then adjusted to around 4.0 with 1 mM sodium hydroxide solution, at which point the solution was clear and pale yellow. Finally, the post-reaction solution was incubated at 37 °C for 1 h to obtain CuNCs.

Under stirring at room temperature, AlCl3·6H2O solution (1 mL, 50 mM) was added to the prepared CuNCs solution (10 mL), and stirred for 2 h to obtain CuNCs@Al3+. CuNCs@Al3+ powders were obtained by lyophilization and subsequently dispersed in deionized water. The prepared CuNCs@Al3+ concentration was 1 mg/mL, and the solution was stored in a 4 °C refrigerator for future use.

The solution of CuNCs@Al3+ and CDs was mixed in 1.2 mL Tris-HCl buffer, and the PL ratio was 4:1. Then Na2S standard solutions (0–50 μM) were added and incubated for 5 min. Fluorescence spectra were recorded under 330 nm excitation. All fluorescence spectral data were measured in triplicate.

Whatman NO.1 filter paper was cut into 1 cm circular discs with a hole punch and placed in the prepared ratiometric fluorescent probe solution. Then, the filter paper was soaked for 30 min and then dried naturally in the dark. Finally, the fluorescence test strips were obtained. After drops of Na2S solution were added to test strips, a smartphone was used to take pictures of the fluorescent test strips after the reaction with different concentrations of S2−. Then the RGB value of the image was recognized by color picker. Finally, a data analysis model was established to achieve quantitative visualization detection of S2− in accordance with the relationship between RGB value and concentration.

The morphology of CuNCs, CuNCs@Al3+ and Si-CDs were performed by TEM. Fig. 2 A shows CuNCs are spherical and have good dispersion. The particle size is mainly distributed in 1.9–3.0 nm, and the spacing corresponds to the crystal plane of copper (111) [34]. Fig. 2B shows the TEM image of CuNCs after adding Al3+. Theatrically, larger aggregates composed of a large number of CuNCs were formed when Al3+ was present. The results confirmed that Al3+ drove the aggregated self-assembly of CuNCs. The distribution of Si-CDs is relatively uniform and spherical in Fig. 2C, with a lattice spacing of 0.20 nm corresponding to the crystal plane of (100) of graphite [35].

Fig. 2D shows FT-IR characterization of CuNCs@Al3+ and Si-CDs. The absorption peak located at 1400 cm−1 and the broad absorption peak at 3373 cm−1 are attributed to the bending and stretching vibrations of O–H, and the characteristic absorption peak of carbonyl group appears around 1650 cm−1, which indicates that a large amount of –COOH. The presence of absorption bands associated with –COOH and –NH2 groups in the FT-IR spectrum confirmed the presence of the protecting ligands of GSH. In addition, the characteristic peak of –SH stretching vibration disappears at 2522 cm−1, which proves the formation of covalent bond (Cu–S-R) between GSH and CuNCs@Al3+. The characteristic peak located at 3441 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibrations of N–H and O–H. The absorption peaks at 2937 cm−1 and 1436 cm−1 are attributed to C–H unsaturated stretching vibration and C–H bending vibration, respectively. The peak at 1650 cm−1 is characteristic of the N–H bending vibration, and the strong signal at 1369 cm−1 is attributed to the C–N stretching vibration. The characteristic peaks at 1126 cm−1 and 1022 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibrations of Si–O and C–N in the Si–OH group, respectively. The characteristic peak at 920 cm−1 is the OH stretching vibration in the carboxylic acid. The above results indicate the presence of abundant –COOH and –OH on the surface of Si-CDs, which endows them with good water solubility and facilitates their application as luminescent probes in aqueous media.

The chemical structure of CuNCs@Al3+ and SiCDs were performed by XPS. Fig. 3 A shows the presence of six elements C, N, O, S, Cu, and Al. There are two distinct peaks at 932.3 and 952.2 eV in Fig. 3B, attributed to Cu2p3/2 and Cu2p1/2 of Cu (0) and Cu (I) [36]. The absence of characteristic peaks around 942.0 eV rules out the presence of Cu (II). Al2p has only one characteristic peak at 74.8 eV, which is attributed to Al3+ (Fig. 3C). This means there is only Al3+ in the CuNCs@Al3+ complex, indicating that Al3+ do not participate in the redox reaction of Cu under acidic or basic conditions. There are four distinct peaks at 284, 398, 531, and 101 eV eV, attributed to C1s, N1s, O1s, and Si2p (Fig. 3D). Four peaks at 283.9, 284.6, 285.4, and 287.4 eV indicated the bonds of C are C–Si, C–C/C=C, C–N and C–O/C=O (Fig. 3E). Three peaks at 101.2, 101.9, and 102.4 eV indicated the bonds of Si are Si–C, Si–N and Si–O (Fig. 3F). Two peaks at 530.8 and 531.6 eV and one peak at 532.6 eV indicated the bonds of Si are O–Si, and O–C (Fig. S1A). Three peaks at 398.1, 399.1, and 399.7 eV indicated the bonds of N are N–Si, C–N–C, and amino (Fig. S1B). These results are consistent with the FT-IR analysis.

The optical properties of CuNCs@Al3+ and Si-CDs solutions were investigated. CuNCs@Al3+ has weak absorption around 260 and 310 nm. The main excitation band of CuNCs@Al3+ is around 310 nm, and the maximum emission wavelength is around 610 nm (Fig. 4 A). The obtained Si-CDs have an absorption band at 280 nm. The main excitation band of Si-CDs is around 340 nm, and the maximum emission wavelength is around 458 nm (Fig. 4B) The fluorescence of Si-CDs/CuNCs@Al3+ was also investigated. The CuNCs@Al3+ solution is golden yellow and Si-CDs solution exhibits blue fluorescence under UV light. However, Si-CDs/CuNCs@Al3+ solution exhibits magenta fluorescence (Fig. 4C).

In addition, fluorescence stability of Si-CDs and CuNCs@Al3+ was also investigated. The photobleaching resistance of Si-CDs was tested by irradiating Si-CDs solution under UV light for 5 h, and measuring the change of fluorescence intensity every 30 min the fluorescence intensity of Si-CDs remained almost unchanged, indicating their resistance to UV photobleaching (Fig. S2A). In addition, PL intensity was measured after storing the Si-CDs at room temperature for three months, which remained almost constant. To prove the fluorescence stability of CuNCs@Al3+, PL intensity of CuNCs@Al3+ was measured every 24 h (Fig. S2B). The results showed PL intensity of CuNCs@Al3+ remained basically unchanged for 10 days, indicating that CuNCs@Al3+ had good stability. Compared with CuNCs@Al3+, CuNCs had an 80% decrease in PL intensity after only 1 day. In addition, weak red fluorescence made it difficult to visualize S2−.

It has been reported that the thiol-terminated CuNCs are uniformly dispersed in aqueous solution with low fluorescence, which is not conducive to their practical application. To improve their fluorescence properties, metal cations were investigated to drive the AIE self-assembly of CuNCs. The effects of 16 kinds of metal ions on the fluorescence of CuNCs were investigated including Al3+, Ce3+, Co2+, Mn2+, Na+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Mg2+, Hg2+, Ag+, Ni2+, Ba2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Cd2+, and Pb2+ (Fig. S3). Except for Al3+ and Ce3+, other ions did not lead to significant enhancement of fluorescence. This may be due to the strong coordination of Al3+ with GSH on the surface of CuNCs, which leads to the efficient aggregation of CuNCs. The large number of coordination bonds confined the molecular vibrations and rotations of GSH, thereby suppressing nonradiative relaxation. These resulted in a reduction in the non-radiative decay rate to enhance the emission. This was also proved by the change of distribution state of CuNCs in TEM images before and after the addition of Al3+ (Fig. 2A and B). Consequently, Al3+ ions were selected to induce CuNCs for AIE self-assembly. With the increase of Al3+ concentration, the fluorescence gradually increased and then reached equilibrium (Fig. 4D and E). Therefore, the optimal concentration of Al3+ is 150 μM. The color of CuNCs changed from dark to bright red under 365 nm UV light. Compared to the dark CuNCs, the CuNCs @Al3+ show bright red. Compared with the case without Al3+ addition, the fluorescence QY of CuNCs increased from 0.65% to 5.29% due to AIE induced by Al3+. In addition, the fluorescence lifetime was increased from 2.37 μs to 10.96 μs (Fig. 4F). It needs to be emphasized that the aggregation of CuNCs enhances the interaction between GSH and Cu nuclei, constrains the molecular vibrations and rotations of GSH, resulting in suppressed radiation-free relaxation [38]. These resulted in strong and longer-lived fluorescence from metal-centered triplet emission. These phenomena confirm Al3+-triggered AIE of CuNCs. The excellent performance regulation of CuNCs by Al3+ provided the possibility for subsequent sensitive detection of S2−.

To maximize the visual effect of detecting S2−, the fluorescence intensity ratios (I458/I610) of dual-emitting fluorescent probes were explored as 1:2, 1:4 and 1:6, respectively. Fluorescence analysis was performed and the color change of the system was observed upon adding different concentrations of S2− (Fig. S4). Color changes from orange-red to light purple to blue can be seen when the ratio is 1:4. However, when the fluorescence intensity ratio was 1:2 and 1:6, the color change of the system was not as wide as that of 1:4. Therefore, 1:4 was chosen as the optimal intensity ratio for subsequent experiments.

In addition, the reaction time of the ratiometric fluorescent probe Si-CDs/CuNCs@Al3+ and S2− was investigated (Fig. S5). With the increase of reaction time, I458/I610 increased gradually. The quenching reaction of CuNCs@Al3+ at 610 nm tends to be stable after 5 min, while S2− does not react with Si-CDs at 458 nm, and the fluorescence intensity constant. Therefore, the optimal reaction time was 5 min.

Fig. S6 shows the change of FL before and after adding S2− in the pH range of 4–11. The surface of CuNCs is negatively charged due to glutathione on the surface of CuNCs under strong acid conditions, which hindered self-assembled CuNCs driven by Al3+ ions. Under strong alkaline conditions, Al3+ in CuNCs@Al3+ easily forms hydroxide precipitation with OH−, which also hindered self-assembled copper nanoclusters. The difference in I458/I610 of Si-CDs/CuNCs@Al3+ before and after addition of S2− was the largest at pH = 7. Therefore, pH = 7 was selected as the optimal pH of the reaction system.

The effect of S2− on the PL intensity of CuNCs@Al3+ and CuNCs was systematically investigated. To evaluate the sensitivity to S2− quenching, the same concentration of S2− to both fluorescence systems was added. The evaluation shows that the CuNCs@Al3+ fluorescence system has a larger signal change and a quenching efficiency as high as 80%. This quenching efficiency is 20% higher than that of the CuNCs fluorescence system, indicating that the CuNCs@Al3+ system is more sensitive as an “on-off” fluorescence platform for S2 detection (Fig. 5 A). In addition, the obvious color change opens up the possibility of introducing smartphone visualization analysis later. These results highlight the enhanced sensitivity performance of the constructed AIE system of CuNCs (“on-off” fluorescence platform) for S2− detection compared to the non-AIE system of CuNCs.

Fluorescence quenching mechanism of the reaction system was firstly explored by UV spectra. Fig. 5B shows the UV absorption peaks of S2−, Si-CDs/CuNCs@Al3+, and Si-CDs/CuNCs@Al3+ composites combined with S2−. No new absorption peaks of Si-CDs/CuNCs@Al3+ appeared after the addition of S2−. This indicates that the quenching process between S2− and Si-CDs/CuNCs@Al3+ belongs to dynamic quenching. In addition, the fluorescence emission peaks of CuNCs@Al3+ do not overlap significantly with the UV absorption peaks of S2−, so there is no fluorescence energy resonance transfer between S2− and CuNCs@Al3+.

The changes of the fluorescence lifetime of CuNCs@Al3+ before and after adding S2− were investigated. The fluorescence lifetime of CuNCs@Al3+ is 10.96 μs, and after the interaction with S2−, the fluorescence lifetime is shortened to 8.14 μs (Fig. 5C). The results show that the fluorescence lifetime of CuNCs@Al3+ occurs after adding the quencher S2− The change proves that the quenching mechanism is a dynamic quenching mechanism.

Furthermore, the interaction between S2− and Si-CDs/CuNCs@Al3+ was illustrated by zeta potential measurements. The surface of Si-CDs is negatively charged with a zeta potential of −9.83 eV, which is determined by the functional groups on the surface of Si-CDs. The surface of CuNCs is negatively charged due to glutathione on the surface of CuNCs. However, under the action of Al3+, the surface of CuNCs@Al3+ is positively charged with a zeta potential of 28.34 eV. The zeta potential of the ratiometric fluorescent probe Si-CDs/CuNCs@Al3+ is 13.41 eV. After S2− was added to the probe, the zeta potential of Si-CDs/CuNCs@Al3+ became −5.24 eV, indicating that the charge transfer between S2− and CuNCs@Al3+ occurred. Due to the negatively charged nature of S2− and its higher adsorption energy than neutral thiol groups, such as the sulfhydryl group in glutathione, S2− has a greater effect than other sulfhydryl amino acids. Therefore, we speculate that the covalent bond (Cu–S-R) formed between GSH and CuNCs@Al3+ is broken by S2−, which forms a stable compound Cu2S (Ksp = 2.5 × 10−48) with Cu (I) [10]. The addition of S2− caused a double hydrolysis reaction of Al3+ and S2−, which turns Al3+ into Al(OH)3. This may hinder the Al3+-driven AIE self-assembly of CuNCs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent sensing of S2− using spectrophotometer
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence responses of Si-CDs/CuNCs@Al3+ incubated with different concentrations of S2− for 5 min were measured. The PL of CuNCs@Al3+ with orange emission at 610 nm gradually quenched with increase of S2−, while PL of blue-emitting Si-CDs at 458 nm remained almost unchanged (Fig. 6 A). There is a good linearity relationship between the value of S2− and I458/I610 value when the concentration is 0.5–40 μM (R2 = 0.997) with low LOD (0.16 μM) in Fig. 6B. This is two orders of magnitude lower than the WHO guideline value (14.8 μM) for S2− in fresh water, demonstrating its potential in practical use [6].

The solution color upon S2− reacting with system was acquired using a smartphone camera (Fig. 7 A). Under the action of S2−, the color of solution gradually turned from orange-red to light pink to light purple, and finally to blue. Then, the RGB value of the color is obtained through the color recognition extractor of the smartphone, and the B/R analysis model is established with the concentration of S2− (Fig. 7B). Through data analysis, it is finally found that there is a good linearity relationship between the value of S2− and the B/R value when the concentration is 1–40 μM (R2 = 0.995) with low LOD (0.31 μM). This indicates that smartphone recognition of the fluorescence color of the Si-CDs/CuNCs@Al3+ ratio probe solution can achieve rapid quantitative detection of S2−.

For portability, ratiometric fluorescent test strips were prepared to rapidly detect S2− on site. When different concentrations of S2− solutions were dripped onto fluorescent test strips, color change of the prepared fluorescent test strips was basically consistent with that of solution in Fig. 7C. The RGB value of the fluorescent test strips after reaction was identified by a smartphone. The B/R value was linearly fitted with the S2− concentration from 1 μM to 40 μM (R2 = 0.988) with a low LOD (0.42 μM) in Fig. 7D. Results prove that the ratiometric fluorescent test strip combined with a smartphone can realize the rapid visual and quantitative detection of S2− on site.

The fluorescence response of Si-CDs/CuNCs@Al3+ solution was tested by adding 25 different substances as interferences to investigate its selectivity (Fig. 8 ). I458/I610 enhanced by 3.78 times when 30 μM S2− was added. However, it was much less affected than S2− when different substances were added, including 500 μM of Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Ba2+, 100 μM of F−, Cl−, NO3 −, Ac−, Br−, I−, SO4 2−, PO3 2−, HPO4 2−, H2PO4 −, CO3 2−, ascorbic acid (AA), glucose (GLU), zero-valent sulfur (S), thiane sulfur (THS), methionine (MET), cysteine (CYS), cystine (CYT), and glutathione (GSH). It is worth noting that S2− have a greater effect than other sulfhydryl amino, due to the negatively charged nature of S2− and their higher adsorption energy than neutral thiol groups [39]. When 30 μM S2− was added to the above interfering substances, I458/I610 was significantly enhanced, indicating that the coexisting substances did not interfere much with S2−. This may be related to the strong binding ability of S2− and Cu (I) from CuNCs@Al3+ mentioned above. The fluorescent probe proved to have good anti-interference performance against other substances.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of S2− in actual water samples
ce:section.ce:para: The established method was used to detect S2− in local river water to evaluate the accuracy and practicality of visual detection. First, S2− response in aqueous medium was investigated. S2− was not detected in the pretreated river water samples. For the spike recovery experiment, 1, 10, and 40 μM of S2− standard solution were added to river water samples and detected with the prepared Si-CDs/CuNCs@Al3+ solution. The recoveries of S2− detected by fluorescence spectrophotometer and smartphone were 96.0%–108.7%, and the relative standard deviations were 2.1%–4.7% (Tables S1–S2). Results show that Si-CDs/CuNCs@Al3+ solution can reliably and effectively detect S2− in actual water samples by using spectrometer and smartphone.

The ability of Si-CDs/CuNCs@Al3+ test strips to detect S2− in real samples was investigated. After dropping S2− on Si-CDs/CuNCs@Al3+ test strips, the color changed significantly (Fig. S7). Changes were similar to those when detecting the standard sample, indicating that Si-CDs/CuNCs@Al3+ test strips could visually test S2− in actual sample. The RGB value of the color of the fluorescent test strips was obtained through the color recognition extractor of the smartphone program, and the recovery of S2− were calculated to be 95.0%–110.4%, respectively (Table S3). Results indicate practical application potential of smartphone integrated test strip based on Si-CDs/CuNCs@Al3+.

The results were verified by standard methylene blue method [37]. The results consistently show that the proposed method is accurate (Table S1). The established method was compared with other methods for detecting S2− (Table S4). The LOD and linear range of the construction method are comparable to the other methods. In terms of time response, methods 1, 6, and 7 are longer than this method. Although methods 2, 3, and 5 avoid the shortcoming, they have other deficiencies. For example, method 2 has a larger LOD, while the raw materials of methods 3 and 5 contain precious metals, making the probes costly. The fast response may be due to the synergistic effect based on the double hydrolysis reaction and the interaction of the covalent bonds of S2− and CuNCs. The ratiometric strategy makes the probes resistant to external factors, resulting in more reliable results. The introduction of smartphones has made visual analysis faster, easier and more accurate. The presence of AIE improves the sensitivity of the sensing system and produces more obvious color changes, which is conducive to visual discrimination.

In conclusion, a visual and on-site fluorescence sensing system with high sensitivity and selectivity was successfully established for detecting S2−. The ratiometric fluorescence sensor was fabricated using CuNCs@Al3+ combined with Si-CDs. This strategy shows some attractive features for S2− detection, as shown below. (1) CuNCs@Al3+ and Si-CDs are mixed in proportion to generate two fluorescence responses as S2− recognition units. (2) The visual detection of S2− has obtained a large range of continuous color changes, which are easy to identify with the naked eye. (3) A smartphone integrated test strip sensing platform was developed to realize intelligent and sensitive detection of S2−. The proposed smartphone-integrated strip sensing platform enables the quantitative point-of-care detection of S2− in real water samples. This research provides new horizons for on-site rapid monitoring environmental samples.

Chenhui Yin: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - review & editing. Ting Liu: Data curation, Investigation. Meng Wu: Methodology. Haochi Liu: Investigation. Qijun Sun: Conceptualization, Xue Sun: Conceptualization. Na Niu: Funding acquisition, Writing - review & editing, Supervision. Ligang Chen: Funding acquisition, Writing - review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This project was funded by the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (2572021AW42), the Project program of Tianjin Key Laboratory of Food Quality and Health, Tianjin University of Science and Technology, P. R. China (No. TJS202102), Fundamental Research Funds for Provincial Colleges and Universities in Heilongjiang Province (2021-KYYWF-0583), 111 Project (B20088), and Heilongjiang Touyan Innovation Team Program (Tree Genetics and Breeding Innovation Team).

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2022.340460.
